text: in particular, about how such indeed liminal fantasies and carnivalesque impulses became genealogically tied to rationalization processes (Szakolczai 2013a). As Shakespeare intuited so famously, within a horizon of modernity all the world has become one stage. Hence, by celebrating the liminal as a going beyond modernity, we have indeed been celebrating the very most problematic aspect of the modern, and we have failed to come to grips with some of its underlying mechanisms. The problem with this identification of the liminal as a sheer, liberating force can best be grasped by reference to Plato’s ontology, presented in his late writing, Philebus. Plato divides all that exists (23C) into four classes: the limited, the unlimited, their combination, and the causes of their combination. The rest of the dialogue is largely concerned with arguing for the superiority of the third, mixed class over the first two ‘pure’ cases (while saying little about causes, left for the follow-up dialogue, Timaeus). In other words, if the modern Kantian worldview was obsessed with the limits of thought, constraining all human experience into locked and ultimately empty categories, then postmodern thinking jumped into the other abyss, celebrating anything that was without limits. On this note, Plato would not have hesitated to place postmoderns alongside the Sophists, who constantly showed ignorance of the need for limits; as he would have had little patience with those ‘rational thinkers’ who do not recognize the centrality of Into Liminality 11 limit-experiences. To think with liminality very basically means to realize that human life is organized as a precarious balance between the limit and the limitless. ‘Freedom’ is an empty word outside such a balance of existence, or, as Voegelin called it, a ‘balance of consciousness’. The Guiding Idea of this Book: The Implosion of Liminality in Modernity The contemporary scene seems to be characterized by an increasingly ambivalent attitude toward liminality: on the one hand a fear of liminal experiences as truly personality transforming events, on the other hand a celebratory stance towards any kind of liminality. On the one hand, and as stressed very much by Foucault (1979), the modern world was always characterized by closing off everything that lay beyond the boundaries of rationality. This hallmark of modernity as excluding such ‘limit’ experiences was perhaps expressed most clearly by Kant. On the other hand it has also been argued, and by a series of thinkers, writers and artists, that the modern world is somehow a ‘carnival’, a grotesque, never-ending comedy, in which ‘limit-experiences’ turn into norm, a frenzy that never really cools down. An underlying question permeating this book is this: How are we to understand this mixture of rationality and comedy? How, and in what ways, have ‘social dramas’ and liminal experiences become part of the modern figuration? The guiding idea behind the writing of this book can be expressed in simple terms: it is the idea that liminality somehow came to occupy a more and more central place within the space of modernity, a process which is currently accelerating to the point of absurdity. There are two larger dimensions to this process, spatial and temporal. Modernity as the Centralization of Liminality Having essentially to do with an in-between position, the point of departure for any analysis of liminality must be spatial. Not surprisingly, van Gennep’s book from 1909 contains a crucial, full chapter on ‘the territorial passage’, the title of Chapter 2 which followed immediately upon his initial classification of rites. In this chapter, van Gennep describes this situation in Europe before the demarcation of national boundaries: ‘Each country was surrounded by a strip of neutral ground which in practice was divided into sections or marches [...] Zones of this kind were important in classical antiquity, especially in Greece, where they were used for market places or battlefields’ (1960: 17–18). The two examples invoked here by van Gennep are absolutely fundamental, as they touch upon the origins of the two institutions that would come to give shape to European modernity: market economy and warfare. Both activities, warfare and trade, were in most cultural contexts of which we have record, carried out in liminal spatial figurations, in what van Gennep also calls ‘neutral zones’. Such zones were in-between areas, standing outside the normal social order, and therefore required Liminality and the Modern 12 a cultural elaboration, a ritual passage. This also meant that activities taking place here could under no circumstance be carried into the ordinary life of the surrounding settlements, as this would undermine the stability and very texture of social life. This is, of course, in a nutshell, what later happened, and with effects on meaningful social relations that we still need to decipher. Entertainment industries thriving on sex, comedy and violence now operate from the very spatial centres of cities, in what Simmel fittingly called the ‘economic jungle of the Metropolis’. The empty space of borderlands has settled within the neutral spaces of Metropolitan shopping areas, where strangers mingle freely. Never before in the history of the world have non-places occupied so much space (Benko and Strohmayer 1997: 23; Augé 1995). Meanwhile, warfare has itself become permanentized in both space and time (Mallksö 2012). Surprisingly enough, this danger was signaled with great clarity by Plato (1995), in his discussion of the khora in Timaeus. The question relates to one of Plato’s main concerns in his late writings, namely the productive powers of nothingness, and how non-being turns into being. Here Plato reluctantly had to go beyond Parmenides, who had always insisted that nothing can grow out of nothing, and that one should therefore not talk about nothingness. But evidently Plato sensed that the end of the classical world critically had to do with exactly such a proliferation of non-space (in which the Sophist thrives). Khora is a void, an abyss, in which things can reproduce themselves infinitely, it ‘re-flects’ like a mirror that is not affected by the image it reflects. Khora is without limit, without bottom, without ground. The Khora is nothingness and
